message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Particulate respirators such as N95 masks are an essential component of personal protective equipment (PPE) for front-line workers. This study describes a rapid and effective UVC irradiation system that would facilitate the safe re-use of N95 respirators and provides supporting information for deploying UVC for decontamination of SARS-CoV-2 during the COVID19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To assess the inactivation potential of the proposed UVC germicidal device as a function of time by using 3M® 8211 - N95 particulate respirators inoculated with SARS-CoV-2.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A germicidal UVC device to deliver tailored UVC dose was developed and snippets (2.5cm
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    ) of the 3M-N95 respirator were inoculated with 10
                    <jats:sup>6</jats:sup>
                    plaque-forming units (PFU) of SARS-CoV-2 and were UV irradiated. Different exposure times were tested (0-164 seconds) by fixing the distance between the lamp (10 cm) and the mask while providing an exposure of at least 5.43 mWcm
                    <jats:sup>-2</jats:sup>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The current work is broadly applicable for healthcare-settings, particularly during a pandemic such as COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Not applicable.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome(s) and Measure(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Primary measure of outcome was titration of infectious virus recovered from virus-inoculated respirator pieces after UVC exposure. Other measures included the method validation of the irradiation protocol, using lentiviruses (biosafety level-2 agent) and establishment of the germicidal UVC exposure protocol.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    An average of 4.38×10
                    <jats:sup>3</jats:sup>
                    PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    (SD 772.68) was recovered from untreated masks while 4.44×10
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    (SD 203.67), 4.00×10
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    (SD 115.47), 1.56×10
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    (SD 76.98) and 4.44×10
                    <jats:sup>1</jats:sup>
                    PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    (SD 76.98) was recovered in exposures 2s,6s,18s and 54 seconds per side respectively. The germicidal device output and positioning was monitored and a minimum output of 5.43 mWcm
                    <jats:sup>-2</jats:sup>
                    was maintained. Infectious SARS-CoV-2 was not detected by plaque assays (minimal level of detection is 67 PFUml
                    <jats:sup>-1</jats:sup>
                    ) on N95 respirator snippets when irradiated for 120s per side or longer suggesting 3.5 log reduction in 240 seconds of irradiation.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A scalable germicidal UVC device to deliver tailored UVC dose for rapid decontamination of SARS-CoV-2 was developed. UVC germicidal irradiation of N95 snippets inoculated with SARS-CoV-2 for 120s per side resulted in 100% (3.5 log in total) reduction of virus. These data support the reuse of N95 particle-filtrate apparatus upon irradiation with UVC and supports use of UVC-based decontamination of SARS-CoV-2 virus during the COVID19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Scalable, effective, and rapid decontamination of SARS-CoV-2 contaminated N95 respirators using germicidal ultra-violet C (UVC) irradiation device
